title: Three parts in one place
checkLabels.t10: The counter does not leak out
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The code works. The three parts of each loop are scattered across three different places.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
why.frozen: Your code never stops. Check the third part — does the counter change.
hint2.while-left: Both of them, not just one.
hint2.wrong-income: Seven days at forty cups at three lev.
hint3.wrong-income: If you get 960, the loop runs eight times. Check where the counter starts and where it stops.
why.day-leaked: The counter still exists after the loop.
hint2.day-leaked: Declared inside the brackets of for, it lives only in the loop. Declared outside, it stays forever.
hint3.day-leaked: typeof day should give undefined. If it gives number, the counter is declared in the wrong place.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Two loops, both working. The week's income and the seats in the shop.
blocks.1.text: Each has a start, a condition and a change — but the three are in three different places. The start above the loop, the condition in the brackets, the change somewhere in the body.
blocks.3.text: The same loop, but the three parts sit together, in the order they happen.
blocks.5.items.0: where it starts — runs once, before anything
blocks.5.items.1: how long it continues — checked before every repetition
blocks.5.items.2: what changes — runs after every repetition
blocks.7.text: With while the third part is inside the body, among the other lines. If the body is twenty lines, it gets lost among them — and it is exactly what causes infinite loops.
blocks.8.text: With for it cannot be forgotten. Its place is visible, and empty if it is missing.
blocks.9.quote: You see the whole loop on one line and know exactly how many times it will run.
blocks.11.text: Declared with let inside the brackets, it exists only for the duration of the loop. After the closing brace it is gone.
blocks.12.text: That is a good thing. The counter is the loop's internal business and has no reason to outlive it. With while it is declared outside and stays forever — one more name hanging around.
blocks.14.text: Rewrite both loops with for. The answers stay the same.
blocks.15.text: The last line checks whether the counter leaked out. After the rewrite it should give undefined.
